video_title: The New Rule for Picking AI Winners | The a16z Show
gist: An a16z growth investor (David) argues AI is not in a bubble because the sector is supply-constrained, not demand-constrained, with frontier labs (OpenAI + Anthropic) on track for ~$200B combined revenue run-rate by end of 2026 against <5% enterprise diffusion. The central new investing rule: back companies that are 'in the token path,' pick the leader in any space with strong founders, and accept that who captures value is now far harder to predict as company half-lives shorten.

insights.claim: OpenAI and Anthropic are each adding more revenue per month than Meta, Google, or Microsoft, and their combined revenue run-rate could hit ~$200B by end of 2026.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Frontier-lab revenue is scaling faster than the hyperscalers' added revenue; watch for $200B run-rate as a milestone that validates AI infrastructure CapEx and private-lab valuations.
insights.quote: Anthropic and OpenAI are adding more revenue per month than Meta, Google, or Microsoft.

insights.claim: Actual diffusion of AI into the real economy is less than 5%, concentrated in coding and tech-forward firms, with most enterprise functions barely using the capability.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: The gap between current lab revenue and <5% diffusion implies a very large runway; treat enterprise AI adoption as early-innings, not late-cycle.
insights.quote: actual diffusion of this technology into the real economy is tiny. It's like less than 5%.

insights.claim: The upper bound on AI spend can be sized against Fortune 500 / S&P 500 collective profits of ~$2 trillion/year; two labs at $200B run-rate alone equals ~10% of that profit pool.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Framework for bounding total AI TAM: enterprises must fund AI from profit, so $2T profit pool sets the ceiling and forces either price hikes or labor restructuring.
insights.quote: they generate like 2 trillion of profit per year at the collective

insights.claim: The model companies are already adding more revenue than the entire public software universe combined, making prior SaaS market sizes look small.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Implies relative reallocation away from legacy public software toward AI labs; bearish read-through for incumbent SaaS revenue capture.
insights.quote: the model companies are adding more than the entire public software universe in terms of revenue added, you know, combined

insights.claim: Cost pressure has already forced 'open source and local' to matter sooner than expected, because AI cost is hitting enterprise buyers in the face.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Watch open-source and on-prem/local inference as cost-driven beneficiaries; budget pressure pulls demand toward cheaper alternatives faster than the consensus timeline.
insights.quote: cost is going to hit us in the face and make them really important sooner than we thought

insights.claim: The takeoff seen in coding is beginning in other white-collar functions like legal, and is expected to spread across enterprise functions and verticals over the next 12 months.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Vertical AI applications in legal and other white-collar functions are the next adoption wave; an early entry point before diffusion broadens.
insights.quote: it's starting to happen in legal

themes.theme: The new picking rule: token path + leader-in-space

insights.claim: The number-one screen for backing an AI company now is whether it is 'in the token path' — directly in the flow of model inference rather than legacy-style software.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Concrete investability filter: prioritize companies whose value is tied to token consumption; legacy-generation software outside the token path faces budget compression.
insights.quote: right now, you have to be in the token path. Like that is the number one thing that we're looking to for our companies.

insights.claim: The Khosla-derived philosophy is to back the leader in any space with strong founders and tailwinds; if the space works and you have the leader, great, and if it fails with the leader, 'no harm, no foul' — the only bad outcome is the space working and backing the wrong company.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Reframes early-stage risk: the error to avoid is picking the loser in a winning space, not betting on a space that fails; informs how to read a16z's concentrated AI bets.
insights.quote: The the bad box of what I described is the space works out and we picked the wrong one.

insights.claim: A low loss ratio is a red flag, not a badge of honor — a VC who has 'never lost money on a deal' is taking too little risk and behaving like a PE firm.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Investors evaluating GPs should be wary of suspiciously low loss ratios; in venture, appropriate loss rates signal genuine risk-taking and power-law upside.
insights.quote: one of his uh big points of pride is he's never lost money on a deal. And we're like, that's not... a point of pride. Like that's a horrible data point.

insights.claim: Model companies are 'legging their way up into the application layer' as their biggest lever to drive stickiness, after prior cycles oscillated between 'models are everything' and 'models are just APIs.'
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Labs moving into apps raises competitive risk for AI application startups; assess whether a portfolio app can defend against the lab that supplies its tokens.
insights.quote: now we're back in this moment where the model companies are kind of legging their way up into the application

themes.theme: Shrinking company half-lives and value-capture uncertainty

insights.claim: 40% of the companies on Forbes' AI 50 list dropped off from one year to the next, implying an incredibly short half-life for AI startups.
insights.speaker: host, LP / fund-of-funds investor
insights.so_what: Rapid turnover means today's leaders may not be tomorrow's; underwrite AI positions for high obsolescence risk and avoid over-anchoring on current leaders.
insights.quote: 40% of the companies that were on that list last year dropped off

insights.claim: First movers no longer reliably capture the economic value of a market — as Google wasn't the first search engine and Facebook wasn't the first social network — and predicting AI winners has become much harder.
insights.speaker: host, LP / fund-of-funds investor
insights.so_what: Discount first-mover premium in AI; the value-capture winner may emerge later, favoring diversified exposure over concentrated early bets.
insights.quote: Google wasn't the first search engine, Facebook wasn't the first social media site

insights.claim: The single biggest unknowable driving AI value capture is the market structure of the model labs: a few frontier labs means higher token prices, while ~five frontier labs means lower token prices.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Track the number of frontier labs as the key variable; lab concentration determines token pricing power, labor-restructuring pace, and where ecosystem value accrues.
insights.quote: If there's a couple at the frontier, token prices will probably be higher. If there are five at the frontier, token prices will probably be lower.

insights.claim: There is a tremendous amount of inelasticity for frontier intelligence right now — buyers are voraciously demanding the absolute frontier rather than substituting cheaper prior-generation models.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Frontier demand inelasticity supports lab pricing power today; an eventual 'optimization phase' (substituting cheaper models) is the risk to watch and may arrive sooner than expected.
insights.quote: there's a tremendous amount of inelasticity for frontier intelligence right now

themes.theme: Token economics, China, and open source

insights.claim: Per-token cost for like-for-like capability is falling more than 10x year-over-year, yet aggregate dollar spend on frontier tokens is rising even faster because of demand.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Falling unit cost is not deflationary for the sector yet — volume growth swamps price declines; revenue keeps rising despite cheaper tokens.
insights.quote: The sort of per token cost like for like is going down more than 10x year over year. But the appetite for tokens on the frontier is massively exceeding that in terms of dollars.

insights.claim: Leading Chinese LLMs are roughly six months behind US frontier capability but about 10x cheaper, posing a classic innovator's-dilemma threat where an 80%-capability product at 10% of cost erodes the frontier over time.
insights.speaker: host, LP / fund-of-funds investor
insights.so_what: Cheaper Chinese/next-tier models could capture share for tasks not needing the absolute frontier; a structural margin risk to US frontier labs over the decade.
insights.quote: the leading LLMs in in China are probably six months behind where we are in the US in terms of the capability of their models, but they're 10x cheaper

insights.claim: Distilling a big model costs on the order of 2% of its pre-training cost; if that remains possible, it bodes well for open source, and the big labs actively don't want their models distilled.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Distillation economics (~2% of training cost) are the swing factor for open-source viability; labs' defenses against distillation determine whether open source stays competitive.
insights.quote: it probably costs in the order of like 2% of the actual training cost pre-training cost of a model to distill it

insights.claim: The threshold for a top-1% venture exit has roughly 10x'd in 24 months — from $10B (2020-2024) to $20B (early 2026) to $32B for closed exits — and could exceed $100B by September once OpenAI/Anthropic are counted.
insights.speaker: host, LP / fund-of-funds investor
insights.so_what: Exit magnitudes are exploding; LPs should expect returns concentrated in a handful of mega-outcomes, reshaping fund-sizing and ownership-target math.
insights.quote: between 2020 and 2024, top 1% exit started at $10 billion... it's now at $32 billion. So, we've 10xed... over the space of kind of 24 months

insights.claim: The combination of the largest AI companies may be larger than the entire Russell 2000, and the sum of all VC-backed IPOs over the last 6 years (~$1T) is likely smaller than any one of three expected large AI IPOs.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: A few coming AI IPOs could individually dwarf six years of prior VC-backed IPO value combined; size public-market absorption and index effects accordingly.
insights.quote: if you sum all of them up they're a little over a trillion dollars... that's probably going to be smaller than any of the three of the large IPOs uh that we expect to happen

insights.claim: Companies like Wiz and Cursor went from nothing to $30B and potentially $60B in only 4-6 years, illustrating that the pace of value creation, not just the size of outcomes, is accelerating.
insights.speaker: host, LP / fund-of-funds investor
insights.so_what: Compressed value-creation timelines mean capital must be deployed and ownership locked in earlier; named comps (Wiz, Cursor) anchor the speed of the new cycle.
insights.quote: Particularly something like Wiz and Cursor, you'd... like 4, 5, 6 years and... to get from nothing to well, 30 billion dollars and then potentially 60 billion dollars

insights.claim: AI companies hit 'big company problems' very early — Cursor has billions in revenue while still tiny and young — forcing VCs to build large platforms (pricing, sales-scaling, international, channel) that founders now demand earlier in a company's life.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Explains a16z's platform scale-up and why large established firms are winning early AI breakouts; founder preference for scaled platforms is a structural advantage over emerging managers.
insights.quote: cursor as an example is you know, billions of dollars of revenue and they're very small and it's very early in their life

themes.theme: Valuations, loss ratios, and the bubble debate

insights.claim: AI early-stage loss ratios are currently in single-digit percentages versus a historical ~60% loss ratio for early-stage funds, an unsustainable dynamic that mirrors 2021's 'peak emerging manager' zero-loss illusion — except now driven by established firms.
insights.speaker: host, LP / fund-of-funds investor
insights.so_what: Today's abnormally low AI loss ratios will revert toward historical 60%; underwrite for mean reversion and rising failure rates as 'laws of gravity reassert.'
insights.quote: when we look at our early stage funds, there's a 60% loss ratio... If I was looking at the loss ratio of the last couple of years in the AI space... it's probably single figures percentages... And like that's not sustainable.

insights.claim: A UK VCA audience poll found 80% think AI valuations are too high and ~6% too low — read as roughly correct, since ~80% of AI companies are likely overvalued and a small undervalued subset will become the leaders worth multiples of today's price.
insights.speaker: host, LP / fund-of-funds investor
insights.so_what: Supports a diversified-basket approach to AI: most names overvalued, a few massively undervalued; LPs gain edge from breadth rather than single-company selection.
insights.quote: 80% said too high, about 6% said too low... 80% of companies probably are overvalued today... a small subset of those companies that are massively undervalued

insights.claim: AI is not in a bubble now because it is supply-constrained (compute, memory, data centers, power) rather than demand-constrained — unlike classic bubbles defined by excess supply destroying economics.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Reframes the bubble debate around supply scarcity; the bubble risk only materializes if supply catches up or a model-efficiency breakthrough causes oversupply.
insights.quote: I feel pretty confident saying that we're not in a bubble right now. I'm less confident... that we won't be in a bubble 3 years from now.

insights.claim: Data center capacity at scale cannot be obtained until late 2028 / early 2029, with US buildout running about a year behind schedule across the entire supply chain, partly due to TSMC's deliberate restraint.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Multi-year supply scarcity through 2028-29 supports pricing and the no-bubble thesis; bullish for data-center supply-chain and power names, and a constraint on AI rollout pace.
insights.quote: You can't get data center capacity at scale until late '28, early '29 right now

insights.claim: If ~$5 trillion of CapEx is spent on buildout over four to five years, $1-2 trillion of revenue return is a reasonable expectation, and two labs alone reaching $200B run-rate this year should make that equation comfortable.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Provides a sanity-check ratio for the AI-CapEx debate: $5T spend against $1-2T revenue; the labs' trajectory is the evidence cited to justify the spend.
insights.quote: if we spend 5 trillion of CapEx... can you get one or two trillion dollars of revenue as a as a return on that?

insights.claim: The main thing that could end supply scarcity and risk a bubble is massively smaller models from an algorithmic breakthrough — inspired by the human brain's far greater learning efficiency — making AI much less token-consumptive.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: A model-efficiency step change is the key tail risk to the supply-constrained bull case; it could flip the market to oversupply and compress infrastructure economics.
insights.quote: the one thing that could shift that would be, you know, massively smaller models. You know, probably comes from like an algorithmic breakthrough of some sort.

themes.theme: Public markets, indexes, and consumer upside

insights.claim: Excluding the data-center supply chain, almost no public companies are growing fast — the Mag Seven and software companies are all growing sub-30%, with Palantir nearly the only name growing ~70%.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Scarcity of high-growth public names argues for bringing hyper-growth AI companies public; flags Palantir as an outlier and the data-center chain as the current growth pocket.
insights.quote: the mag seven are all growing sub 30% at this point... Palantir is really the only one growing, you know, whatever 70%

insights.claim: Bringing AI companies public while still in hypergrowth is good for investors, and index inclusion is likely — a needed counter to public-company count shrinking by half over 20 years.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Expect large AI IPOs and index inclusion to broaden retail/passive ownership; some rotation out of existing holdings to make room, but markets can absorb it.
insights.quote: the number of public companies has shrunk in half... this is going to be a good shot in the arm to bring some very high-growth interesting stuff into the public markets

insights.claim: The biggest AI outcomes will likely come from the consumer side via a shift in 'time spent,' reversing the prior decade in which big tech captured consumer attention and made competition extremely hard.
insights.speaker: guest, a16z growth investor (David)
insights.so_what: Watch consumer AI for the largest outcomes; a reallocation of consumer time/attention away from incumbent big-tech platforms is the thesis to track.
insights.quote: we're going to see a shift in time spent, you know, consumer attention... which I think will probably create, you know, really extraordinary outcomes
